topic: A homeowner learns their house was a former crime scene
raw_output:
[story 1]

The realtor had been overly enthusiastic, practically vibrating with excitement about the "character" of the old Victorian. I’d shrugged off his warnings about the neighborhood's history; I just wanted a quiet place to raise my family. But then, while unpacking the dusty attic, I found it: a section of the plaster in the study was strangely marred. It wasn't just water damage; there were faint, almost invisible impressions beneath the peeling wallpaper, shapes that didn't belong to any structural flaw. My jaw tightened as I ran a fingertip over the area, feeling a prickle of unease crawl up my arm. I tried to rationalize it—old settling, maybe a bizarre historical stain—but the way the plaster had been disturbed felt deliberate. I spent the next week cross-referencing old city records, the thrill of the discovery warring with a growing, cold knot in my stomach.

[story 2]

I kept checking the security footage, running the playback over and over, hoping for a glitch, a misinterpretation. The house had been vacant for years, a fixer-upper with a reputation for oddities. I was just trying to confirm the strange markings I’d noticed near the foundation—scratches that looked too precise to be accidental. When the contractor finally came in to assess the basement, he paused. He didn't say anything immediately, just stood there, his shadow stretching long in the dim light. When he finally spoke, his voice was low, measured, and utterly devoid of surprise. "This place has seen things," he murmured. I leaned back against the doorframe, crossing my arms tightly over my chest, my gaze fixed on the floorboards. I wanted to believe it was just old building decay, but the silence in the room seemed to amplify the sheer impossibility of his statement.

[story 3]

It started subtly. A misplaced object in the hallway, a faint smell that wouldn't dissipate, a certain heaviness in the air whenever I entered the master bedroom. I dismissed it as stress, as the residual energy of a poorly maintained property. But then I found the hidden panel behind a bookcase. Inside, tucked beneath brittle newspaper clippings, was a small, tarnished metal object—a key, perhaps. My hands trembled slightly as I lifted it. The weight of it felt significant, heavy with unspoken history. I spent hours trying to determine its origin, researching local lore, but every avenue led to dead ends. I kept looking over my shoulder, convinced that whatever this house held, it wasn't something I was equipped to handle.
